coverage_notes: **High-confidence regression only** — mostly read-only tools, honest skips, or ask turns with low flake (see prior analysis). **Moved out:** chain-dependent, write-heavy, or project-sensitive cases are listed in [`regression-watchlist.json`](regression-watchlist.json). Sources still include run-22/run-20/mixed/run-13/fine-tune where applicable; see Tool Iteration Log. **Expectation:** these should usually reach `run_finished` with few `tool_finish_false` on a typical Blank-style project; API/editor drift still possible.

turns.request: What engine/plugin line does this project claim in the little text manifest at the project root? One or two sentences.
If this project has a level sequence or cinematic timeline asset, open it; if there isn't one, say you couldn't find one—don't invent one.
Last check: what's selected in the editor right now, and one sentence on whether the level and content browser state look sane for handoff.
In two or three sentences: are we in the normal editor (not PIE), what level feels open if any, and is anything selected in the level or Content Browser?

Multi-step viewport exercise: (1) read the active level editor viewport camera transform and FOV, (2) apply a small orbit or dolly so the view visibly changes, (3) briefly summarize the numbers you read and confirm the camera moved. Actually drive the editor—don’t invent numbers.

Show me roughly the first 50 lines of Config/DefaultEngine.ini (or the closest standard engine config path for this project). Mention one non-empty setting line you saw; if the file is missing or unreadable, say what happened.
Search the game source for a symbol tied to this game module (e.g. Blank, or the C++ module name you infer). List up to five file paths you get back, or say you found nothing.
In one short paragraph: which category felt heaviest—viewport/camera, blueprint reading, physics trace, file/config read, asset search, or planning—and did anything fail outright?
Materials still look like mushy gray—hang back until the editor's actually finished cooking whatever it's cooking.
I'm back after coffee—where's the editor camera roughly right now (position/rotation), and can you swing the view a little left or right so I can tell it actually moved?
From how I'm looking at the level, if I pointed straight ahead into the scene, would I hit anything solid? What is it in plain words—no level edits.
In one sentence: what should strict deterministic tests verify after an agent request?
Create a blueprint actor named BP_MassDummy_Controller in the interaction logic area you picked and open it.
Now add BP_MassDummy_Controller_Helper and keep it in the same neighborhood as the main controller.
Create BP_MassDummy_DoorRelay and BP_MassDummy_DoorRelay_Debug in a location that stays organized with the controller assets.
Tell me exactly where those four blueprints live and then open the debug relay one.
Make a duplicate of the main controller for experiments with a clear prototype-style suffix, and keep it near the original.
Add another sibling blueprint called BP_MassDummy_Controller_ProtoB in the same area.
Open BP_MassDummy_Controller_ProtoB and give me a short note that you opened the right one.
Create BP_MassDummy_RouteNode_A and BP_MassDummy_RouteNode_B under the runtime side of that structure.
Duplicate BP_MassDummy_RouteNode_A into a variant for fallback logic and use a name that clearly communicates fallback behavior.
Give me a compact inventory of everything created in this session that starts with BP_MassDummy_, including object paths.
Based on where you placed these assets, does your layout look coherent and scalable, or should one part be reorganized?
Create one more blueprint actor named BP_MassDummy_SignalHub in the same feature area and open it.
